is an [inner-east](/wiki/Eastern_Suburbs_(Sydney) "Eastern Suburbs (Sydney)") [suburb](/wiki/Suburb "Suburb") of [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"), in the state of [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales"), Australia. It is immediately south-east of the [Sydney central business district](/wiki/Sydney_central_business_district "Sydney central business district") in the [local government area](/wiki/Local_government_in_Australia "Local government in Australia") of the [City of Sydney](/wiki/City_of_Sydney "City of Sydney"). Surry Hills is surrounded by the suburbs of [Darlinghurst](/wiki/Darlinghurst "Darlinghurst") to the north, [Chippendale](/wiki/Chippendale,_New_South_Wales "Chippendale, New South Wales") and [Haymarket](/wiki/Haymarket,_New_South_Wales "Haymarket, New South Wales") to the west, [Moore Park](/wiki/Moore_Park,_New_South_Wales "Moore Park, New South Wales") and [Paddington](/wiki/Paddington,_New_South_Wales "Paddington, New South Wales") to the east and [Redfern](/wiki/Redfern,_New_South_Wales "Redfern, New South Wales") to the south.[[3]](#cite_note-3) It is often colloquially referred to as "Surry".

**Surry Hills** is an [inner-east](/wiki/Eastern_Suburbs_(Sydney) "Eastern Suburbs (Sydney)") [suburb](/wiki/Suburb "Suburb") of [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"), in the state of [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales"), Australia. It is immediately south-east of the [Sydney central business district](/wiki/Sydney_central_business_district "Sydney central business district") in the [local government area](/wiki/Local_government_in_Australia "Local government in Australia") of the [City of Sydney](/wiki/City_of_Sydney "City of Sydney"). Surry Hills is surrounded by the suburbs of [Darlinghurst](/wiki/Darlinghurst "Darlinghurst") to the north, [Chippendale](/wiki/Chippendale,_New_South_Wales "Chippendale, New South Wales") and [Haymarket](/wiki/Haymarket,_New_South_Wales "Haymarket, New South Wales") to the west, [Moore Park](/wiki/Moore_Park,_New_South_Wales "Moore Park, New South Wales") and [Paddington](/wiki/Paddington,_New_South_Wales "Paddington, New South Wales") to the east and [Redfern](/wiki/Redfern,_New_South_Wales "Redfern, New South Wales") to the south.[[3]](#cite_note-3) It is often colloquially referred to as "Surry".

Surry Hills is bordered by [Elizabeth Street](/wiki/Elizabeth_Street,_Sydney "Elizabeth Street, Sydney") and [Chalmers Street](/wiki/Chalmers_Street "Chalmers Street") to the west, [Cleveland Street](/wiki/Cleveland_Street,_Sydney "Cleveland Street, Sydney") to the south, South Dowling Street to the east, and [Oxford Street](/wiki/Oxford_Street,_Sydney "Oxford Street, Sydney") to the north. [Crown Street](/wiki/Crown_Street,_Sydney "Crown Street, Sydney") is a main thoroughfare through the suburb with numerous restaurants, pubs and bars. [Central](/wiki/Central,_New_South_Wales "Central, New South Wales") is a locality in the north-west of the suburb around [Central station](/wiki/Central_railway_station,_Sydney "Central railway station, Sydney"). [Prince Alfred Park](/wiki/Prince_Alfred_Park "Prince Alfred Park") is located nearby. [Strawberry Hills](/wiki/Strawberry_Hills "Strawberry Hills") is a locality around Cleveland and Elizabeth Streets and [Brickfield Hill](/wiki/Brickfield_Hill "Brickfield Hill") to the east of that.

A multicultural suburb, Surry Hills has had a long association with the [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_people "Portuguese people") community of Sydney.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Surry_Hills&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The first land grants in Surry Hills were made in the 1790s. Major [Joseph Foveaux](/wiki/Joseph_Foveaux "Joseph Foveaux") received 105 acres (0.42 km2). His property was known as Surry Hills Farm, after the [Surrey Hills](/wiki/Surrey_Hills_National_Landscape "Surrey Hills National Landscape") in [Surrey](/wiki/Surrey "Surrey"), England. Foveaux Street is named in his honour.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Commissary John Palmer received 90 acres (360,000 m2). He called the property George Farm and in 1800 Palmer also bought Foveaux's farm. In 1792, the boundaries of the Sydney Cove settlement were established between the head of Cockle Bay to the head of Woolloomooloo Bay. West of the boundary, which included present-day Surry Hills, was considered suitable for farming and was granted to military officers and free settlers.

After Palmer's political failures, his reduced financial circumstances forced the first subdivision and sale of his estate in 1814. [Isaac Nichols](/wiki/Isaac_Nichols "Isaac Nichols") bought Allotment 20, comprising over 6 acres (24,000 m2). Due to the hilly terrain, much of the suburb was considered remote and 'inhospitable'. In the early years of the nineteenth century the area around what is now [Prince Alfred Park](/wiki/Prince_Alfred_Park "Prince Alfred Park") was undeveloped land known as the Government Paddocks or Cleveland Paddocks. A few villas were built in the suburb in the late 1820s. The suburb remained one of contrasts for much of the nineteenth century, with the homes of wealthy merchants mixed with that of the commercial and working classes.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Salvation_Army_-_Surry_Hills_Sunday_morning_service_in_street%2C_Sept_1949%2C_from_Series_02-_Sydney_people_%26_streets%2C_1948-1950%2C_photographed_by_Brian_Bird_%286977871553%29.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:Salvation_Army_-_Surry_Hills_Sunday_morning_service_in_street,_Sept_1949,_from_Series_02-_Sydney_people_%26_streets,_1948-1950,_photographed_by_Brian_Bird_(6977871553).jpg)

A Surry Hills street scene, 1940s

In 1820, Governor Macquarie ordered the consecration of the [Devonshire Street Cemetery](/wiki/Devonshire_Street_Cemetery "Devonshire Street Cemetery"). A brick wall was erected before any interments took place to enclose its 4 acres (16,000 m2). Within a four-year period the cemetery was expanded by the addition of 7 acres (28,000 m2) to its south. A road was formed along the southern boundary of the cemetery in the first half of the 1830s and was called Devonshire Street. The Devonshire Street Cemetery, where many of the early settlers were buried, was later moved to build the Sydney railway terminus. [Central railway station](/wiki/Central_railway_station,_Sydney "Central railway station, Sydney") was opened on 4 August 1906. The area around Cleveland and Elizabeth streets was known as [Strawberry Hills](/wiki/Strawberry_Hills "Strawberry Hills"). Strawberry Hills post office was located at this intersection for many years.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

In 1833, the Nichols estate was subdivided and sold. One purchase was by Thomas Broughton and subsequently acquired by George Hill who constructed Durham Hall on this and adjoining lots. Terrace houses and workers' cottages were built in Surry Hills from the 1850s. Light industry became established in the area, particularly in the [rag trade](/wiki/Rag_trade "Rag trade") (clothing industry). It became a working class suburb, predominately inhabited by Irish immigrants. The suburb developed a reputation for crime and vice. The Sydney underworld figure [Kate Leigh](/wiki/Kate_Leigh "Kate Leigh") (1881–1964), lived in Surry Hills for more than 80 years.

In 1896 [Patineur Grotesque](/wiki/Patineur_Grotesque "Patineur Grotesque") one of Australia's first [films](/wiki/Film "Film") and first comedy routine filmed was shot in Prince Alfred Park by [Marius Sestier](/wiki/Marius_Sestier "Marius Sestier").[[7]](#cite_note-7)

The government enacted legislation during 1945-1951 to require all municipalities to prepare 'town plans' for the purpose of guiding future development for a thirty year period (to 1975). In 1948, there was a proposal for Surry Hills by the Municipal County for Sydney titled Sunny Hills.[[8]](#cite_note-SunnyHills1948-8) This proposed the almost complete demolition of Surry Hills and redevelopment of the street plan to accommodate a new expressway[[8]](#cite_note-SunnyHills1948-8): 27 (carrying the [Eastern Distributor](/wiki/Eastern_Distributor "Eastern Distributor"), at that time proposed as a surface expressway), residential zones dominated by blocks of flats set in parkland and separated zoning. A 300 foot diameter roundabout was proposed for the intersection of Crown Street and Foveaux Street.[[8]](#cite_note-SunnyHills1948-8): 24 This town plan was never implemented, and the City of Sydney Council archivist note states that the Sunny Hills plan was probably only ever an exercise.[[8]](#cite_note-SunnyHills1948-8)

Surry Hills was favoured by newly arrived families after [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") when property values were low and accommodation was inexpensive. From the 1980s, the area was [gentrified](/wiki/Gentrification "Gentrification"), with many of the area's older houses and building restored and many new upper middle-class residents enjoying the benefits of inner-city living. The suburb is now a haven for the upper middle class and young rich.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

### Trams

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Surry_Hills&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Trams")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Elizabeth_St._from_Eddy_Ave._1940s.jpg/250px-Elizabeth_St._from_Eddy_Ave._1940s.jpg)](/wiki/File:Elizabeth_St._from_Eddy_Ave._1940s.jpg)

Tram lines from [Eddy Avenue](/wiki/Eddy_Avenue "Eddy Avenue") running into [Elizabeth Street](/wiki/Elizabeth_Street,_Sydney "Elizabeth Street, Sydney") in the early 1940s

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Wimbo_Park_-_Surry_Hills_Sydney_NSW_-_2026_03.jpg/250px-Wimbo_Park_-_Surry_Hills_Sydney_NSW_-_2026_03.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wimbo_Park_-_Surry_Hills_Sydney_NSW_-_2026_03.jpg)

Light rail corridor in Wimbo park adjacent to Bourke St.

Main articles: [Trams in Sydney](/wiki/Trams_in_Sydney "Trams in Sydney") and [Public transport in Sydney](/wiki/Public_transport_in_Sydney "Public transport in Sydney")

The West Kensington via Surry Hills Line operated from 1881 down [Crown Street](/wiki/Crown_Street,_Sydney "Crown Street, Sydney") as far as Cleveland Street as a steam tramway. It was extended to Phillip Street in 1909, Todman Avenue in 1912, and then to its final terminus down Todman Avenue in 1937. When the line was fully operational it branched from the tramlines in Oxford Street and proceeded down Crown Street to Cleveland Street in Surry Hills, then south along Baptist Street to Phillip Street, where it swung left into Crescent Street before running south along Dowling Street. It passed the Dowling Street Depot, then turned left into Todman Avenue, where it terminated at [West Kensington](/wiki/Kensington,_New_South_Wales "Kensington, New South Wales"). The line along Crown Street closed in 1957, the remainder stayed open until 1961 to allow access to [Dowling Street Tram Depot](/wiki/Dowling_Street_Tram_Depot "Dowling Street Tram Depot"). [Transdev John Holland](/wiki/Transdev_John_Holland "Transdev John Holland") routes 301, 302 and 303 generally follow the route down Crown and Baptist Streets as far as Phillip Street.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## Urban character

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Surry_Hills&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Urban character")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Crown_Street-Campbell_Street%2C_Surry_Hills%2C_Sydney.jpg/330px-Crown_Street-Campbell_Street%2C_Surry_Hills%2C_Sydney.jpg)](/wiki/File:Crown_Street-Campbell_Street,_Surry_Hills,_Sydney.jpg)

Commercial buildings on the corner of [Crown](/wiki/Crown_Street,_Sydney "Crown Street, Sydney") and Campbell Streets

Surry Hills has a mixture of residential, commercial and light industrial areas. It remains Sydney's main centre for fashion wholesale activities, particularly on the western side. The area is also home to a large LGBTQIA+ community, where Sydney's Gay and Lesbian Mardi Gras (Pride Parade) takes place each year.

Surry Hills Markets are held in Shannon Reserve at the corner of Crown and Collins Streets, on the first Saturday of every month,[[11]](#cite_note-11) and the Surry Hills Festival is an annual community event, attracting tens of thousands of visitors, held in and around Ward Park, Shannon Reserve, Crown Street and Hill Street.[[12]](#cite_note-12)
The Surry Hills Library and Community Centre sits opposite Shannon Reserve and houses the local branch of the [city library](/wiki/City_of_Sydney_Library "City of Sydney Library") and the Surry Hills Neighbourhood Centre.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Surry_Hills&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

#### Literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Surry_Hills&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Literature")]

*[The Harp in the South](/wiki/The_Harp_in_the_South "The Harp in the South")* is a novel by [Ruth Park](/wiki/Ruth_Park "Ruth Park"). Published in 1948, it portrays the life of a Catholic Irish-Australian family in Surry Hills, which was an inner city slum at the time. A sequel, *[Poor Man's Orange](/wiki/Poor_Man%27s_Orange "Poor Man's Orange")*, was published in 1949.

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Surry_Hills&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Transport")]

[Central railway station](/wiki/Central_railway_station,_Sydney "Central railway station, Sydney"), the largest station on the [Sydney Trains](/wiki/Sydney_Trains "Sydney Trains") and [NSW TrainLink](/wiki/NSW_TrainLink "NSW TrainLink") networks, sits on the western edge of Surry Hills. Surry Hills is also serviced by [Transdev John Holland](/wiki/Transdev_John_Holland "Transdev John Holland") and [Transit Systems](/wiki/Transit_Systems_NSW "Transit Systems NSW") buses. The [Eastern Distributor](/wiki/Eastern_Distributor "Eastern Distributor") is a major road, on the eastern edge of the suburb. Major thoroughfares are [Crown Street](/wiki/Crown_Street,_Sydney "Crown Street, Sydney"), [Cleveland Street](/wiki/Cleveland_Street,_Sydney "Cleveland Street, Sydney"), Bourke Street and Foveaux Street. Surry Hills is within easy walking distance of the Sydney CBD, and is included in a widening network of cycleways.

Major construction took place on the Surry Hills section of the [CBD and South East Light Rail](/wiki/CBD_and_South_East_Light_Rail "CBD and South East Light Rail") in a project managed by [Transport for NSW](/wiki/Transport_for_NSW "Transport for NSW").[[14]](#cite_note-14) It has been reported that there had been some disruption to local businesses because of the construction work taking place.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

The new light rail service opened in 2019, running between Circular Quay and Randwick[[16]](#cite_note-Light_Rail-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18) with a stop on Devonshire street adjacent to Ward Park and Riley street, Surry Hills.[[19]](#cite_note-19) In 2020 a second service opened running between Circular Quay and Kingsford.[[16]](#cite_note-Light_Rail-16)

### Restaurants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Surry_Hills&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Restaurants")]

Surry Hills boasts a diverse range of cafes and restaurants serving a wide variety of cooking styles and cultures.[[26]](#cite_note-26) The suburb has one of the highest, if not the highest, concentration of restaurants in Sydney.[[27]](#cite_note-27) Local chefs include [Andrew Cibej](/wiki/Andrew_Cibej "Andrew Cibej") and [Bill Granger](/wiki/Bill_Granger "Bill Granger").

### Pubs and bars

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Surry_Hills&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Pubs and bars")]

Because of its industrial and commercial history, the Surry Hills area contains a significant number of [pubs](/wiki/Pubs "Pubs"). The style of pubs range from the [Victorian period](/wiki/Victorian_architecture "Victorian architecture") to [Federation](/wiki/Federation_architecture "Federation architecture") and [Art Deco](/wiki/Art_Deco "Art Deco") pubs from the mid-1900s. Many of these have been refurbished in recent years to include restaurants and modern facilities.

## Population

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Surry_Hills&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Population")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/Sticker_art_in_Sydney_-_2025_10h_-_1.jpg/250px-Sticker_art_in_Sydney_-_2025_10h_-_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sticker_art_in_Sydney_-_2025_10h_-_1.jpg)

[Sticker art](/wiki/Sticker_art "Sticker art"), by the artist "Onnie", on the back of a street sign on Cleveland street, in Surry Hills. Photo taken in 2025.

Demographically, Surry Hills is now characterised as a mixture of wealthy newcomers who have [gentrified](/wiki/Gentrification "Gentrification") the suburb, and long-time residents.

At the [2021 census](/wiki/2021_Australian_census "2021 Australian census"), the population of Surry Hills was 15,828.[[48]](#cite_note-census2021-48) At the [2016 census](/wiki/2016_Australian_census "2016 Australian census"), it had a population of 16,412.[[49]](#cite_note-census2016-49)

In 2021, 68.5% of dwellings were flats, units or apartments, compared to the Australian average of 14.2%. 29.1% are semi-detached terraced houses or townhouses, compared to the Australian average of 12.6%. Only 1.1% of dwellings are separate houses, compared to the Australian average of 72.3%. Surry Hills was categorised as a high wealth area, with a median weekly household income of $2,308, compared to the Australian average of $1,746.[[48]](#cite_note-census2021-48)
Historically, the suburb had an influx of post-war immigrants from Europe, particularly those from [Greece](/wiki/Greek_Australian "Greek Australian"), [Portugal](/wiki/Portuguese_Australian "Portuguese Australian")[[50]](#cite_note-50) and [Italy](/wiki/Italian_Australian "Italian Australian").

In 2021, 48.7% of people were born in Australia. The most common foreign countries of birth were England 6.4%, New Zealand 3.4%, China (excludes SARs and Taiwan) 3.3%, Thailand 3.0% and the United States of America 2.1%. 73.0% of people only spoke English at home. Other languages spoken at home included Cantonese 4.0%, Mandarin 3.0%, Thai 3.0%, Greek 2.0% and French 2.0%.[[51]](#cite_note-Microburbs_2023-51)

47.5% of dwellings have no cars, compared to the Australian average of 7.3%. 11.0% of the population walked to work, compared to the Australian average of 2.5%, and 9.1% travelled to work by public transport, compared to the Australian average of 4.6%. 57.0% worked at home, compared to the Australian average of 21.0%.[[48]](#cite_note-census2021-48)

In 2021 Surry Hills was a significantly more [irreligious](/wiki/Irreligion "Irreligion") suburb than the Australian average. Most (55.3%) reported no religion whilst 8.4% did not answer the question. The most common religions reported were Catholic 14.0%, Buddhism 4.8% and Anglican 4.8%.[[48]](#cite_note-census2021-48)
